quote: Hey, Donna. This is Holland with Ion. I have a request you were looking into solar for your home. How are you doing today?
stewart_read: Holland's intro omits both the form-fill reference and any legitimacy anchor. The schema's standard intro template calls for referencing the form submission ('the solar form you filled out online') to legitimize the call and reduce scam suspicion. Holland's phrasing — 'I have a request you were looking into solar' — is grammatically awkward and provides no trust signal. The Tesla intro variant was not applicable here, but the standard variant's legitimacy beat was skipped entirely. The schema's what_great_looks_like criterion is 'legitimacy established in first 30 seconds' — this intro does not meet it.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have had lead-source context that made the form-fill reference unnecessary. No evidence of this in the transcript.
coaching_implication: Pull the intro beat in the 1-on-1. Ask Holland: 'What does Donna know about why you're calling in the first 10 seconds?' The answer should be: she filled out a form, and Ion is a credible company. Neither is established here. Drill the standard intro template until the legitimacy anchor is automatic.

quote: I'm interested, but I know they're very expensive. So if if this is gonna be very expensive, you could just take me off the list.
stewart_read: This is the call's highest-leverage moment. Donna has just volunteered her bill ($300/month), her motivation (incentives), and her primary anxiety (cost) in a single breath. The schema's objection_inversion move is directly applicable: 'What you can't afford is your current bill.' Holland instead responds with a generic reassurance ('solar is the most affordable it's ever been') that addresses the objection on its own terms rather than flipping it. The $300/month anchor was already in hand — Holland had the tool and didn't use it.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been deliberately soft-pedaling to avoid pressure on a cost-anxious customer. But the flip is not pressure — it's reframing. The soft response left the anxiety unresolved.
coaching_implication: This is the primary flip-opportunity drill for this rep. In the 1-on-1, replay the moment and ask: 'You had her bill. What could you have said that made $300/month the problem, not solar?' Walk through the inversion structure from the schema. The goal is for Holland to internalize the move, not memorize a line.

quote: I see that you you have some trees on the southern side of your home. Are all of those trees still there?
stewart_read: Holland identifies trees on the southern exposure via satellite and probes whether they're still present. Donna confirms they are and that they're tall. This is a qualification risk — southern-side tall trees are a shading variable that could affect system viability or panel count. Holland notes it ('Okay') and moves on without probing further: no follow-up on canopy density, no mention of whether tree removal is planned, no flag for the designer. The probe was initiated correctly but not completed.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been deferring the shading assessment to the designer, which is within setter scope. But the absence of any follow-up question or flag means the designer receives no context on a known variable.
coaching_implication: Ask Holland: 'What does the designer need to know about those trees?' The answer is: height, canopy density, and whether removal is planned. If Donna had mentioned tree removal, that would have been an enthusiasm signal (improved sun exposure). As it stands, it's an unresolved qualification variable. Coach Holland to either probe one level deeper or explicitly flag shading variables in the handoff note.

quote: I remember it being very, very expensive.
stewart_read: This is the second touch of Donna's cost anxiety pattern. She's not just raising a price objection — she's sharing a memory of being priced out of something she wanted. That's an emotional disclosure, not a negotiating position. Holland's response ('there's been lots of upgrades and technology and updates and whatnot') is accurate but impersonal. It addresses the factual content of the objection while missing the emotional register entirely. A brief acknowledgment of the frustration before pivoting to the technology update would have cost nothing and built trust.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have read this as a factual objection requiring a factual response. The transcript doesn't show overt emotional distress from Donna. But the 'very, very expensive' repetition signals emphasis, not just information.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Holland: 'When Donna said she remembered it being very, very expensive — what was she feeling?' The goal is to get Holland to hear the emotional content under the factual statement. A suggested rephrase: 'Yeah, that's a real frustration — you were interested and the price shut the door. A lot has changed since then, and I think you'll be surprised when you see the actual numbers.' Then move to the flip.

quote: I'm actually not at my house now. I'm I won't be home till probably Sunday or Monday.
stewart_read: Donna is away from home and her bill is mail-only. Holland correctly probes for an online account (04:55) — Donna has none. At this point the schema's alternative paths are: verbal readback (not applicable — bill is at home), defer to specialist site visit, or lock a structured callback. Holland does none of these. Instead, Holland sends a text and leaves the follow-up entirely unstructured. No callback time is locked, no button-up is executed, and the call ends on a soft mutual agreement with no anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that locking a specific callback time would feel pushy given Donna's travel situation. But the schema's button_up protocol exists precisely for this scenario — a locked time is not pressure, it's respect for Donna's schedule.
coaching_implication: Drill the bill-deferral pivot. When the bill can't be captured live and no online account exists, the move is: lock a specific callback time before hanging up. Suggested structure: 'You'll be home Monday — what time works for a quick 5-minute call so I can walk you through grabbing that bill?' That converts a soft callback into a scheduled touchpoint. Also flag: if this is a paid lead, this is a missed sit pending lead-type confirmation.

quote: If I don't get if I don't call you because sometimes I forget. I get busy with stuff. So just give me a call, and I'll try and I'll pull that up, and I'll see if I can make a copy of it for you.
stewart_read: Holland self-discloses unreliability ('sometimes I forget, I get busy') in the final seconds of the call. This is not rapport — it's rapport damage. It shifts the follow-up burden onto Donna, signals that Holland may not follow through, and undermines the professional credibility Holland spent the call building. The schema's button_up protocol requires a text follow-up promise and a clear next-step anchor. Holland inverts this: instead of 'I'll reach out Monday,' Holland says 'you might need to chase me.'
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been trying to sound relatable or low-pressure. The intent may have been warmth. The effect is the opposite.
coaching_implication: This is a direct coaching moment. Play the clip. Ask Holland: 'What does Donna hear when you say that?' The answer is: 'This person might not call me back.' The fix is simple — own the follow-up. Suggested rephrase: 'I'll give you a call Monday afternoon. If you have the bill handy, we'll get the estimate wrapped up in five minutes.' No self-deprecation, no burden-shifting.

stewart_read: Holland captures the bill ($300/month) and acknowledges it ('300 is kind of expensive, but we can definitely help get that cut down for you'). The capture is complete. The flip is not. The schema's bill_anchoring pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Holland executes ask + acknowledge + generic reassurance. The $300/month figure is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch and it's dropped after one sentence. Donna's cost anxiety, which surfaces twice, could have been resolved here by making $300/month the villain rather than solar the solution.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been saving the flip for after the design was built. But the design can't be built without the bill, and the bill can't be collected until Monday — so the flip opportunity is now, not later.
coaching_implication: This is the bill-anchor drill. In the 1-on-1: 'You said 300 is kind of expensive. What's the next sentence?' Walk Holland through the inversion: the $300/month is the expense solar replaces, not a number to minimize. The emotional alignment-against-the-utility is the move. Holland has the number — coach the pivot.

quote: Okay. Thank you, Helen. Talk to you soon. Bye bye.
stewart_read: Donna closes the call by getting Holland's name wrong ('Helen' instead of 'Holland'). This is a minor signal but worth noting: it suggests Holland's name didn't land firmly enough during the call to stick. More importantly, the call ends with zero button-up elements executed — no appointment datetime, no one-hour block, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, and no structured text follow-up promise. The schema's button_up section lists six required elements; none are present because no appointment was booked. The coaching gap is upstream: Holland should have attempted to lock a Monday callback as a structured appointment rather than a soft agreement.
alternative_hypothesis: No appointment was booked, so button-up protocol technically doesn't apply. But the absence of any structured next-step anchor is the gap — the button-up discipline should have been applied to the callback itself.
coaching_implication: Use this as a teaching moment on what 'booking' means. A Monday callback with no locked time is not a booking — it's a hope. Ask Holland: 'What's the difference between Donna calling you Monday and you having Donna on the calendar for Monday at 2pm?' The answer is the no-show rate. Drill the callback-as-appointment framing.
